stories.headline: DU Pioneers win record 11th national championship at Frozen Four with 2-1 victory over Wisconsin
stories.summary:
The University of Denver men's hockey team claimed its 11th NCAA national championship Saturday afternoon, defeating Wisconsin 2-1 at T-Mobile Arena in Las Vegas. Freshman goaltender Johnny Hicks made 29 saves, backstopping the Pioneers through more than 45 minutes of Wisconsin dominance before DU's offense came alive in the third period.

Senior forward Rieger Lorenz broke the tie at 7:31 of the third period, shoveling home a rebound for his 17th goal of the season. Freshman Kyle Chyzowski scored the championship winner at 14:08, giving the Pioneers a lead they would not relinquish. The victory capped a 17-game winning streak to close the season.

Hicks, who stands just 5-foot-10, finished the season with a 16-0-1 record as the starter and made 49 saves two nights earlier in a double-overtime semifinal win over top-seeded Michigan. DU has now won three of the past five national titles, in 2022, 2024 and 2026, and holds two more championships than any other NCAA program.

stories.headline: JBS, UFCW Local 7 reach tentative deal to end historic Greeley meatpacking strike
stories.summary:
The United Food and Commercial Workers Local 7 and JBS officials announced a tentative collective bargaining agreement Friday, potentially ending a nearly month-long strike at the Greeley beef processing plant. The agreement came after two days of resumed negotiations following a three-week walkout by approximately 3,800 workers.

Workers walked off the job March 16 after union officials said JBS refused to increase minimum wages and committed unfair labor practices during negotiations — claims the company denies. The strike was the first at an American meatpacking plant in four decades and the first-ever walkout at the Greeley facility, which is the city's top employer.

Union leaders did not disclose the terms of the tentative agreement, saying it will be presented to members for a ratification vote Sunday. The Greeley plant, which operates as Swift Beef Co., processes as much as 8 percent of the country's beef supply. JBS did not respond to a request for comment.

stories.headline: Denver suspends all licenses for Nativ Hotel & Gatsby Social after repeated fire safety violations
stories.summary:
The City of Denver has suspended all business licenses for Nativ Hotel & Gatsby Social at 1612 Wazee St. after authorities said the establishment repeatedly ignored fire safety orders and continued operating in defiance of a city-issued stop-work order. The Department of Licensing and Consumer Protection issued the order of summary suspension Friday, shutting down the business immediately and giving it 48 hours to notify current hotel guests.

The Denver Fire Department had been flagging the building for fire code violations since January 2025. A joint inspection in February 2026 found conditions inspectors described as rendering the premises unsafe for occupancy, including a fire alarm system not being monitored with 14 failure signals, blocked exits, propped-open fire doors, sprinkler system violations and improper propane storage. The city cited owner Thierry Rignol with 847 code violations following a March 6 follow-up inspection.

Despite a stop-work order fully reinstated March 26, Denver Police were called to the location twice last weekend and found the bar and nightclub in full operation on both occasions. One call involved a person who threatened a security guard with a gun; the second was for an assault outside the building. The suspension covers the hotel and restaurant liquor license, dance cabaret license, lodging license and retail food license.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers face agonizing cuts to services for disabled people, immigrant children as $1.5B deficit looms
stories.summary:
The Colorado House has begun initial debates over the state's proposed budget, confronting a $1.5 billion structural deficit that would require cuts across dozens of programs, from health care for immigrant children without legal status to assistance for families adopting children, substance abuse programs and raises for state workers. The overall state general fund is expected to total about $17.4 billion for fiscal year 2026-27, an increase of roughly $212 million over the current year — but rising costs in mandatory spending areas consume that increase and more.

The primary budget drivers are the Department of Health Care Policy and Financing, which oversees Medicaid and alone requires $213 million in additional general fund spending, and the Department of Corrections, which needs nearly $69 million more. Thirteen of the 22 departments receiving general fund dollars would see their budgets cut as a result. Lawmakers have also swept cash from fee-based accounts and held back planned cost-of-living raises for state employees to help close the gap.

Lawmakers attribute the structural deficit to the compounding effects of federal tax cuts under last year's H.R. 1 legislation and the growth constraints imposed by Colorado's Taxpayer's Bill of Rights. Rep. Rick Taggart, a Grand Junction Republican on the Joint Budget Committee, said the Medicaid and corrections caseload growth are the undisputed drivers of the crisis.

stories.headline: Tech and business leaders warn Colorado is losing competitive edge, cite regulations and company departures
stories.summary:
A Colorado Chamber of Commerce relocation report identified 98 companies that have left Colorado or bypassed the state since 2019, accounting for 13,607 jobs lost to other states. Separately, more than 230 local tech and business leaders signed an open letter to state lawmakers warning that Colorado's business climate has founders, CEOs and investors viewing the state as less predictable and competitive, pointing to the recent departure of Palantir to Miami as a prominent example.

The chamber's report noted that Colorado ranked in the top 10 states for business for 15 consecutive years on CNBC's annual index before falling to 11th in 2023, then 16th in 2024, before recovering slightly to 11th in 2025. The organization's executive director said Colorado is the sixth-most regulated state in the country, with the volume and pace of new regulations straining businesses.

Signer Dan Caruso, co-founder of Boulder telecom Zayo Group, said the situation had reached a breaking point, calling publicly for political leaders to "course correct." Many signatories also opposed Colorado's artificial intelligence regulation law, set to take effect June 30, which critics say will deter tech investment. The letter warned that Colorado's decades-long technology and business foundation is deteriorating.

stories.headline: Nuggets clinch first-round home court, rest starters in win over Thunder; playoff seeding hinges on Sunday finale
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets rested all five starters Friday night and still pulled away for a 127-107 victory over Oklahoma City, which also rested nine rotation players including MVP Shai Gilgeous-Alexander. The Nuggets' unexpected lineup decision appeared to signal a preference to drop to the No. 4 seed and avoid a first-round matchup with the Minnesota Timberwolves, though coach David Adelman did not confirm that strategy explicitly.

Nikola Jokic was listed out with right wrist injury management, while Jamal Murray, Aaron Gordon, Cam Johnson and Christian Braun were also held out with various ailments. Despite the depleted roster, Denver clinched home-court advantage in the first round of the playoffs with the win.

Denver's final seeding — anywhere from No. 3 to No. 4 in the West — will be determined by Sunday's game at San Antonio and a coinciding Lakers-Jazz contest. A Nuggets win or a Lakers loss would place Denver as the No. 3 seed facing Minnesota; a Nuggets loss combined with a Lakers win would make Denver the No. 4 seed facing Houston. Oklahoma City would be a potential second-round opponent in the latter scenario.

stories.headline: Ex-Broncos first-round pick Paxton Lynch suffers season-ending knee injury in arena football comeback attempt
stories.summary:
Paxton Lynch, the Denver Broncos' first-round draft pick in 2016, has suffered a torn LCL that ended his attempted comeback with the Colorado Spartans of the National Arena League after just two and a half games. Lynch, 32, had joined the Spartans — who pay players $600 per game and play on a 60-yard turf field in Arvada — after going without a professional opportunity at any level for a year and a half.

Lynch was injured in Salina, Kansas, when he planted his right leg as a defender hit him from the left side. The injury prevented him from playing in the Spartans' home debut Saturday. Lynch had described his motivation for the comeback in personal rather than professional terms, saying he wanted to reconcile his identity as a man with his identity as a quarterback after years of bouncing through eight organizations following his two-year stint in Denver.

"I was pissed off, and it sucks. I didn't want it to be like this," Lynch told The Denver Post. Despite the abrupt ending, Lynch said the experience was successful in that he played as himself with full confidence for the first time in years. He said he intends to keep playing until age 45.

stories.headline: Update: Lightning strike victim in Superior identified; family by her side as she remains critical
stories.summary:
The woman struck by lightning Thursday evening while walking on a path in the 1000 block of Lasalle Street in Superior has been identified, the Boulder County Sheriff's Office announced Saturday morning. Her family has been located and is by her side, officials said, though she remains in critical condition.

The woman had no identification on her and her phone was severely damaged by the strike, prompting the sheriff's office to seek the public's help in identifying her Friday. She was identified by 9:54 a.m. Saturday. First responders had revived her at the scene after she was found without a pulse and unconscious.

stories.headline: RTD E and W light rail lines disrupted through Tuesday for Union Station wire replacement
stories.summary:
Regional Transportation District light rail service on the E and W lines will be disrupted through early next week as crews replace 1,600 feet of overhead electrical wires at Denver's Union Station. The work began Saturday morning and normal service is expected to resume Tuesday.

The E line is being rerouted to a downtown loop, detouring from the 10th-Osage Station to the Colfax at Auraria Station before continuing to the 16th and 18th Street stations. Bus bridge shuttles are replacing W line service between the Auraria West Station and Union Station. E line riders can also use the 16th Street FreeRide as an alternative.

stories.headline: DIA ground stop lifted after high winds; more than 200 flights delayed
stories.summary:
The Federal Aviation Administration ordered a ground stop at Denver International Airport Saturday afternoon due to high winds, halting all flights for approximately one hour beginning at 3:49 p.m. Weather sensors at DIA recorded gusts as high as 32 mph with sustained winds reaching 25 mph, according to the National Weather Service.

The ground stop was lifted around 5 p.m. Airlines reported 212 delayed flights and three cancellations as of 5:55 p.m., according to flight tracking site FlightAware. Southwest Airlines had 74 delayed flights, United had 54 and SkyWest had 48.

stories.headline: Colorado home rule debate intensifies as state legislature presses cities on housing density and other policies
stories.summary:
A longstanding tension between Colorado's state government and its home-rule cities has sharpened in recent years as the legislature has passed an increasing number of bills directing local government policy, particularly on housing density. Home rule, enshrined in the state constitution since 1904 following Denver's "City Hall War" of 1894, grants cities authority to govern local affairs through their own charters rather than being subject to direct legislative control.

Colorado's Front Range cities, including Colorado Springs, have pushed back against state laws requiring higher-density zoning that they argue encroach on local authority. The conflict echoes historical disputes in which the governor and legislature wielded direct control over Denver city affairs, including appointing members of municipal boards and commissions, before reformers secured the Home Rule Amendment.

The current standoff pits the governor and majority-Democratic legislature against both conservative and moderate city governments and reflects a broader national debate over whether regional or state intervention is necessary to address housing shortages. Analysts note that more than 125 years of home-rule precedent makes the legal and political questions surrounding the current legislation complex.

stories.headline: Wildfire closes portion of Highway 36 in far eastern Colorado near Kansas border
stories.summary:
A wildfire in far eastern Colorado near the Kansas border forced the closure of a portion of Highway 36 late Saturday, with the fire described as contained by Saturday night though the road remained closed. Details on acreage and the cause of the fire were not immediately available.

The incident follows a period of elevated wildfire activity across Colorado, with multiple fires burning in different parts of the state in recent weeks. Eastern Colorado's plains region is particularly susceptible to fast-moving grass fires during dry and windy spring conditions.

weather.notable: About 15 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: My daughter's teacher called me. She said, 'Your daughter's doing great in school.' I said, 'I know, she gets it from her mother.' She said, 'What do you mean?' I said, 'Well, I've never done great in school.'

topStory.headline: Hungary Votes in High-Stakes Election as Record Turnout Reported Early
topStory.summary:
Hungarian voters turned out in record numbers Sunday for a general election pitting Prime Minister Viktor Orban's Fidesz party against opposition leader Peter Magyar and his Tisza Party. By 11:00 a.m. local time, nearly 38 percent of eligible voters had already cast ballots, surpassing the pace set in the 2002 election — the last time Fidesz lost power.

Orban, who has governed Hungary for 16 years, held a campaign-closing rally in Budapest on Saturday, seeking to energize his base despite polls showing a sustained opposition lead in the months preceding the vote. Supporters at the rally expressed confidence that a so-called silent majority would carry Orban to victory, downplaying the unfavorable polling numbers.

Opposition leader Peter Magyar cast his ballot Sunday and projected confidence, telling reporters the only remaining question was whether Tisza would win a simple or two-thirds parliamentary majority. Magyar, who has campaigned on an anti-corruption platform, also called on voters to report any irregularities at polling stations, warning that election fraud is a serious crime.

The election carries significant implications beyond Hungary's borders. Magyar has signaled a foreign policy posture more favorable toward the European Union than Orban's government, which has maintained close ties with Russia and repeatedly clashed with Brussels. Leading European far-right figures have publicly backed Orban's reelection, underscoring the contest's broader significance for the direction of European politics.
